content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Claude Sonnet 5 launched at a price that looks like a gift. There’s a clock on it, and a tokenizer doing quiet math in the background. Here’s how to actually pocket the savings.
— -
Last week Anthropic shipped Claude Sonnet 5, and the number that got everyone’s attention was the small one: $2 per million input tokens, $10 per million output. For a model that lands within a few points of Opus on hard coding work, that’s a striking sticker.
I moved most of my Opus traffic over the same afternoon. My bill dropped immediately.
But if you stop reading the fine print at the sticker price, you’re going to get a surprise on your September invoice — and it won’t be the good kind. There are two pieces of quiet math running underneath that $2, and they decide whether this launch saves you real money or just *looks* like it did.
Let me show you both, and exactly what to do about them while the window is open.
The savings are real — if you’re overpaying Opus
Start with the part that’s genuinely good, because it is.
Sonnet 5 is Anthropic’s most capable mid-tier model to date. On SWE-bench Pro — the hard agentic-coding benchmark built from actively maintained repos with multi-file diffs and no leaked answers — Sonnet 5 scores 63.2%. Opus 4.8 still leads at 69.2%, and the old Sonnet 4.6 sat at 58.1%. So Sonnet 5 closed most of the distance to the flagship while jumping five points over its own predecessor.
Now look at what each one costs. Opus 4.8 runs $5/$25 per million tokens. Sonnet 5 at standard rates is $3/$15 — about 40% cheaper per token. During the introductory window through August 31, it’s $2/$10, roughly 60% cheaper.
> If you’ve been routing routine pull requests, refactors, and code review through Opus out of habit, you’re paying flagship prices for work a mid-tier model now handles.
That’s the whole opportunity in one sentence. For a lot of teams the money isn’t hiding in some clever prompt trick — it’s sitting in a model-selection default nobody has revisited since the last release. A code-review pass that costs you a dollar on Opus can cost forty cents on Sonnet 5, and the reviewer can’t tell the difference on most routine diffs.
This is the boring, unglamorous win: audit what you’re actually sending to Opus, and move everything that doesn’t need 69.2% down to the model that does 63.2% for half the price.
The first piece of quiet math: the tokenizer
Here’s where the sticker price starts lying to you.
Sonnet 5 ships with a new tokenizer — the component that chops your text into the billable units called tokens. The new one is hungrier. The same prompt, the same file, the same conversation now gets sliced into roughly 30% more tokens than it did on Sonnet 4.6. Depending on the content, independent tests put the inflation anywhere from near-zero on plain prose to 35%, even 41%, on code and structured data.
Sit with what that means. Price per token is only half of your bill. The other half is how many tokens your text becomes. Anthropic can hold the per-token rate perfectly flat and your cost per actual task still climbs, because each task now spends more tokens to say the same thing.
> Per-token parity is not per-task parity. The rate card can say one thing while your invoice says another.
So when you compare Sonnet 5’s future $3/$15 to Sonnet 4.6’s $3/$15 and conclude “same price,” you’re comparing the wrong unit. Measured per task — per pull request, per document, per agent run — the same workload on Sonnet 5 can land 0–35% higher than it did on 4.6, purely from the tokenizer. Code-heavy workloads sit at the painful end of that range.
If you build or automate anything on these models — and if you’re reading this, you probably do — this is the kind of unglamorous plumbing detail that quietly decides your margins. If you’d rather learn to measure and control this stuff than get surprised by it, that’s most of what we do inside [No Cøde Mafia](https://www.skool.com/the-modern-warrior-free-5965), my free community for shipping AI tools and workflows without writing code.
The second piece: the September 1 step-up
Now add the clock.
That $2/$10 is introductory pricing. On September 1, it becomes $3/$15 — a 50% jump in the per-token rate, on the exact same traffic you’re running today.
Stack the two together and the picture sharpens. Today you’re paying $2/$10 on a hungrier tokenizer. In September you’ll pay $3/$15 on that same hungry tokenizer. The rate rises 50%, the token count is already inflated, and the two compound. One analysis pegged the effective landing spot at 20–35% above where you started — while the rate card still reads $3/$15, identical to old Sonnet 4.6, as if nothing happened.
That’s the trap in a sentence: the price you’re celebrating in July is the cheapest this model will be, and the “unchanged” price in September is not unchanged in practice.
[Image suggestion: a split invoice graphic — left side “July: $2/$10 sticker,” right side “September: $3/$15 + ~30% more tokens,” with the real per-task cost curve rising across both. Caption: The rate card barely moves. Your bill does.]
What to actually do with this — a 30-minute plan
None of this means Sonnet 5 is a bad deal. It means the deal is time-sensitive and unit-sensitive, and a little work now protects you later. Here’s the sequence I ran.
First, pull one week of your model usage and find every request going to Opus. For each, ask: does this task genuinely need 69.2%, or is it routine? Route the routine work to Sonnet 5. That’s where the 40–60% lives.
Second, measure cost per task, not per token. Take five representative jobs — a code review, a refactor, a doc summary, a typical agent run — and log the total tokens each consumes on Sonnet 5 versus your old model. Now you’re comparing invoices, not stickers.
Third, do your Opus-to-Sonnet migration before September 1 so you bank two months at intro rates and give yourself a clean baseline before the step-up hits.
Fourth, trim your token count where the tokenizer punishes you most— long system prompts, redundant context, giant pasted files. On a hungrier tokenizer, prompt discipline is worth more than it used to be.
The contrarian turn: is this a price cut or a stealth hike?
Here’s the argument I take seriously, because it’s the strongest one against everything above.
Critics have pointed out that this fits a pattern: keep the headline per-token number flat or low, change the tokenizer so the same work costs more, and let the invoice climb while the rate card provides cover. Read that way, Sonnet 5 isn’t a discount — it’s a price increase wearing a discount’s clothes, and the “60% cheaper” framing is marketing.
I think that critique is half right, and the half it gets right matters. Anthropic absolutely benefits from customers anchoring on the sticker and not the tokenizer. If you do nothing, read nothing, and just trust “$2 is cheap,” you may well pay *more* per task than you did last quarter. The burden is on you to verify.
But here’s where I land: the savings are real for the specific move of shifting Opus work down to Sonnet 5. Even at full $3/$15 standard rates with the hungrier tokenizer, you’re comparing against Opus at $5/$25 — the gap is wide enough to survive a 35% token inflation and still come out clearly ahead. The trap isn’t the model. The trap is treating a per-token sticker as a per-task promise, and doing nothing before September. Verify per task, migrate before the step-up, and the win holds.
Takeaways
1. Move routine Opus traffic to Sonnet 5 now — that’s where the 40–60% savings actually live.
2. Measure cost per task, not per token. A new, hungrier tokenizer adds ~30% (up to 41% on code) tokens to the same work.
3. The $2/$10 intro price ends August 31. On September 1 it becomes $3/$15 — a 50% rate jump on identical traffic.
4. Compounded, the tokenizer plus the step-up can land you 20–35% above your July cost while the rate card looks unchanged.
5. Do the migration and baseline before September 1 to bank intro rates and see the real numbers.
The launch is a genuine opportunity and a quiet trap at the same time, and which one you get depends entirely on whether you read past the sticker. The model is better and, for the right traffic, cheaper. The invoice will still tell the truth in September — the only question is whether you saw it coming.
So: do you actually know what your AI work costs per task, or only per token?
— -
